body.p: In this chapter, we want to build an XSLT transformation that, while traversing an XML tree, sends requests to an XQuery script on a MarkLogic serverScraper Application. Via a GET request, a <title> field is transmitted for persistence in an ML collection.

section.title: App Server Authentication
section.p: To realize this scenario, we need to set the permissions in MarkLogic so that web requests are accepted without authentication. This means our application is intended more for internal use.
Since the Saxon XSLT processor currently delegates URI resolution to the Java URI Resolver Saxon URI Resolver, which does not yet support authentication with credentials in the URL, as in
we need to disable authentication for our MarkLogic web app.
To do this, we set the authentication setting of our App Server to application-level and assign the default user role to an admin user.

fig.title: Setting MarkLogic App Server Authentication
fig.desc: In the App Server configuration you can set the level at which the access mechanism should apply. Here we set [[code:application-level]] with an admin user to disable authentication for the intranet.

section.p: A more sophisticated configuration is described in the chapter Document Permissions in MarkLogic .

section.title: XML Input
section.p: To make the experiment a bit more exciting, we use XML streaming on a Wikipedia dump with 5.3 GB file size Wikipedia dumps. The XML looks as follows:
We want to store all titles in a database, so we match on the <title> element.

section.title: XSLT Transformation
section.p: The streaming transformation using the Iterator concept looks like this:
section.pre: template name="main"> <xsl:source-document href="{$input-file}" streamable='yes'> <result> <xsl:iterate select="page/title"> <xsl:variable name="json-call" select="json-to-xml( unparsed-text( concat($server-url,'/scrap-title.xqy?', 'title=',encode-for-uri(.))))"/> <state> <xsl:sequence select="$json-call/descendant::*[@key='state']/text()"/> </state> </xsl:iterate> </result> </xsl:source-document> </xsl:template>
section.p: Here, individual <state> elements are output within a result structure with a <result> element.
The content of these elements is the return value of a web request via the fn:unparsed-text() function.
A parameter title is passed to the script scrap-title.xqy:
If everything goes well, the transformation should be completed after about half an hour. The
collection /wikimedia-titles should contain many entries, with filenames like:
